Based on the provided evidence, we reaffirm our position that \boxed{D}, referring to clot solubility, is the least likely to be abnormal in disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC).
Several pieces of evidence reinforce this view. Firstly, the literature highlights that D-dimer levels generally elevate substantially in DIC due to the extensive breakdown of clots caused by accelerated fibrinolysis. Since clot solubility refers to the ease with which clots break down, it follows that in situations marked by vigorous fibrinolysis as seen in DIC, clot solubility would actually enhance rather than diminish unless some unusual circumstance were present.
Secondly, while other tests such as prothrombin time (PT) and activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) may occasionally show normalization or slight deviations due to compensatory mechanisms or sample handling nuances, these variations don't negate the fact that DIC fundamentally disrupts normal hemostasis. Thus, PT and APTT can sometimes give misleading impressions of stability whereas D-dimer continues to track the relentless destruction of clots.
Lastly, the consistent observation across multiple studies confirming elevated D-dimer concentrations in DIC underscores its reliability as a marker for ongoing coagulative/fibrinolytic processes. This robust correlation strengthens our argument that clot solubility (\boxed{D}) remains largely invariant compared to other tested parameters whose behaviors fluctuate unpredictably during the tumultuous milieu created by DIC.
Therefore, based on this comprehensive review bolstered by substantial empirical backing, we stand firm in asserting that clot solubility is exceptionally resistant to alteration in DIC, making it distinct among available options for lab findings.
